abstract: On June 16, 2026, the European Parliament voted to approve changes to the EU AI Act's compliance timeline. Many readers now assume the high risk deadline has settled at December 2027. It has not, not yet. This article explains what the EU AI Act requires, why the high risk category matters, what the June 2026 vote did and did not accomplish, and what organizations in Austria and the wider European Union should do while the legal process finishes. It draws on primary sources from the European Parliament, the Council of the European Union, and the Official Journal, alongside legal analysis from international law firms tracking the file in real time.
body:
\## Introduction



If you do not work in technology law, the phrase EU AI Act may mean little beyond a sense that Europe regulates artificial intelligence strictly. That impression is correct. The Act, formally Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, is the first comprehensive law anywhere that regulates artificial intelligence as a general category rather than one sector at a time. It entered into force on August 1, 2024, and rolls out in phases, giving companies, regulators, and standard setting bodies time to prepare for each stage rather than facing every rule at once.



This staged structure is where the current public confusion lives. On June 16, 2026, the European Parliament voted 423 in favour to 57 against, with 174 abstentions, to approve amendments pushing back one of these stage dates by sixteen months. Within days, headlines described the deadline as delayed, in the past tense, as though settled. It is not. A vote in the European Parliament is a necessary step toward changing the law. It is not, by itself, a change in the law.



This article asks two questions and answers both honestly. First, as of today, is the original August 2, 2026 deadline for high risk AI systems still the legally applicable one. Second, what should an organization building or deploying AI systems in Austria, or anywhere in the EU, do between now and whichever deadline ends up applying. Answering either requires understanding what high risk means under the Act, since that category is what this entire debate concerns.



\## The EU AI Act's Risk Pyramid



The EU AI Act does not regulate all AI systems the same way. It sorts them into four tiers based on potential for harm, and the obligations a system faces depend entirely on which tier it falls into.



\[[figure-1]]



At the top sits unacceptable risk: practices the Act bans outright, such as social scoring by public authorities and manipulative AI that exploits a person's vulnerabilities. There is no compliance pathway here, only the choice not to build the system.



Below that sits high risk, the category this article concerns. These systems are not banned, but face the Act's heaviest obligations: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, human oversight, and logging. Annex III lists the use cases involved: employment decisions such as hiring or performance evaluation, biometric identification, access to education, creditworthiness assessment, and certain uses in law enforcement and border management. If your organization uses AI to screen job applicants, score loan applications, or evaluate students, this tier almost certainly applies to you.



Limited risk sits below that, where the main obligation is transparency, a chatbot must generally disclose that the user is talking to AI rather than a person. At the bottom, the largest tier by far, sits minimal risk: spam filters, AI powered game opponents, and recommendation engines, with no special obligations at all. This article concerns the high risk tier specifically, since that is the tier whose timeline just became a matter of public confusion.



\## What the Original Law Required, and When



Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 was never designed to apply in one moment. The first phase took effect February 2, 2025, banning unacceptable risk practices and introducing a baseline AI literacy obligation. The second took effect August 2, 2025, bringing obligations for general purpose AI models, the large foundation models powering many downstream applications, alongside formal designation of national enforcement authorities in each member state.



The third phase was scheduled for August 2, 2026, the one this article concerns. From that date, providers of high risk AI systems would need to meet the full obligations under Articles 9 through 17 of the Act: risk management systems, data governance, technical documentation, record keeping, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy and robustness requirements. Deployers, rather than providers, would face parallel obligations under Article 26. A fourth phase, covering AI systems embedded in already regulated products such as medical devices, machinery, and lifts, was scheduled for August 2, 2027.



Non compliance is not minor. The Act allows fines of up to 35 million euros or 7 percent of an organization's global annual turnover, whichever is higher, for the most serious violations, a higher ceiling than under the General Data Protection Regulation. By late 2025, it was clear the European Commission, the member states, and many regulated organizations were not going to be ready in time. The harmonized technical standards high risk providers needed were behind schedule, and several member states had not finished designating enforcement authorities. This practical reality led to the events of 2026.



\## What Happened in 2026



On November 19, 2025, the European Commission published the Digital Omnibus on AI, part of a broader package simplifying EU digital regulation, proposing a mechanism to delay the high risk obligations. What followed was a fairly typical, if compressed, EU legislative process. Parliament adopted its negotiating position in March 2026, the Council agreed its own position on March 13, 2026, and formal trilogue negotiations between Parliament, Council, and Commission began shortly after. An initial round on April 28, 2026 ended without agreement, largely over how long the delay should be.



A second round succeeded. On May 7, 2026, Parliament and Council reached a provisional political agreement, confirmed by member state representatives on May 13, 2026. Under it, obligations for stand alone high risk systems under Annex III would move from August 2, 2026 to December 2, 2027, a deferral of sixteen months, and obligations for high risk systems embedded in regulated products would move from August 2, 2027 to August 2, 2028. The agreement also added a new prohibition banning AI systems that generate non consensual intimate imagery, sometimes called nudification tools.



A provisional political agreement between negotiators is not a change in the law. It still needed formal approval. On June 2, 2026, Parliament's Internal Market and Civil Liberties committees approved it 93 in favour, 4 against, 15 abstentions, clearing it for a full Parliament vote. On June 16, 2026, Parliament held that vote and approved the amendments 423 in favour, 57 against, 174 abstentions.



\[[figure-2]]



\## What Still Has Not Happened



A vote by the European Parliament is one of two approvals required under the EU's ordinary legislative procedure. The Council of the European Union must also formally adopt the text. Only after both institutions adopt it does the amendment get published in the Official Journal, and only three days after that publication does it actually enter into force.



As of writing, the Council has not yet completed formal adoption, and the text has not been published. Several law firms tracking this file closely, including White and Case and Gibson Dunn, have been explicit on this point in client briefings: the new dates are not binding until publication occurs, and the original August 2, 2026 deadline formally remains in place until then.



In practice, almost everyone involved, including the Council itself, expects formal adoption and publication before August 2, 2026, since that was the point of compressing the negotiation into this timeframe. But expected and law are not the same word, and the gap between them is exactly where compliance risk lives.



\## Why This Gap Matters, Even If the Outcome Is Predictable



It would be reasonable to ask why this matters if the delay is all but certain to be finalized in time. Two reasons. First, the harmonized technical standards organizations need to demonstrate compliance, whichever deadline applies, are themselves behind schedule, and detailed implementation guidance may not arrive until close to whatever the final deadline turns out to be. An organization that pauses governance work now, assuming the deadline has already moved, may end up with no more real preparation time under the new date than under the old one.



Second, formal adoption can slip. Rarely by much, but European legislative timetables are not immune to delay, and a single procedural holdup, a translation issue, a late objection, could push formal adoption past August 2, 2026. If that happened, the original deadline and obligations would apply exactly as written, and any organization that had stopped preparing would be exposed with no buffer at all.



\## What to Do Now



The practical answer is simpler than the legal situation it responds to. Build your AI risk classification, documentation, and governance processes as though August 2, 2026 still applies, because as of today, it does. If the Council formally adopts the amendment and it is published before that date, your organization gains sixteen months of additional runway it did not strictly need to ask for. If the formal steps are not completed in time, your organization is already compliant rather than scrambling in the final weeks of July.

This is the same principle that governs good risk management in any regulated field. When a known deadline has not yet been formally and verifiably superseded, you operate as though it still applies. Treat any extension you are eventually given as a gift, not a guarantee you build your compliance program around in advance.



\## Conclusion



The European Parliament's vote on June 16, 2026 was a genuine and significant step. It reflects the clear intent of both EU legislative institutions to extend the high risk compliance timeline, and it would be a reasonable bet that the extension will ultimately take effect. But a bet is not a fact, and the distinction matters more in regulatory compliance than almost anywhere else, because the cost of being wrong is measured in fines of up to 35 million euros or 7 percent of global turnover.

As of today, the EU AI Act's original high risk deadline of August 2, 2026 remains the legally applicable one. Organizations operating in Austria and across the European Union should plan, document, and govern accordingly, and treat whatever additional time the final legislative steps eventually grant as a buffer they earned, not a deadline they were ever entitled to assume.
citation: Mpofu, E. (2026). The EU AI Act High Risk Deadline: What the June 2026 Vote Changes and What It Does Not.
author: Dr. Ephraim Mpofu

subtitle: What the June 2026 Parliament vote really changed, what still remains legally unresolved, and what organisations should do now.
title: The EU AI Act High Risk Deadline: What the June 2026 Vote Changes and What It Does Not
